# Results

The top model for Snakeden Run based on $AIC_c$ was $S_(time) p_(year) r_(year)$. 
However, estimates for several $r$ parameters failed to converge.
Similarly, the second model (S_(time) p_(.) r_(year)), had similar convergence issues for some $r$ parameters.
Consequently, we selected the third model ($S_(time) p_(.) r_(.)$) for estimating survival and abundance at Snakeden Run.

The top model for Glade Run was ($S_(time) p_(year) r_(year)$).
Some estimates of both $S$ and $r$ parameters failed to converge.
Consequently we selected the second model ($S_(time) p_(year) r_(.)$) for estimating survival and abundance at Glade Run. 

Survival estimates ranged from X to Y at Snakeden Run and X to Y at Glade Run (Table reference and @fig-survival).

Abundance estimates ranged from X to Y at Snakeden Run and X to Y at Glade Run.  
These estimates were directly derived from the release cohort and survival estimates of each site.


Similar to apparent survival, abundance estimates were higher for the Harrison Lake cohorts for both species. 
This difference was less pronounced when reporting abundance estimates as a percentage of the size of the release cohort (@fig-abundance-percent).
In either case, abundance estimates of the uniquely tagged FMCC cohorts were substantially lower than Harrison Lake cohorts.
However, FMCC released a substantial number of non-uniquely tagged and untagged mussels. 
Applying the estimated survival to the entire release cohort of FMCC mussels resulted in abundance estimates ranging from `{r} min_abundance_all_varicosa` to `{r} max_abundance_all_varicosa` for *A. varicosa* and `{r} min_abundance_all_complanata` to `{r} max_abundance_all_complanata` for *E. complanata* (@tbl-abundance_all and @fig-abundance-all).  

# Discussion

Survival was higher at Glade Run for all occasions. This is possibly due to the higher gradient **See report for description here**.
Abundance estimates seem to match naive estimates of abundance based on raw number of mussels observed alive at each occasion. 
However, it is possible that our survival estimates are slightly underestimated compared to true survival.
There was high tag loss (**%here**) at each site.
Individuals with lost tags are modeled as if they were not seen again, which has the effect of underestiamting survival (**reference**) 


Survival was generally higher for Harrison Lake cohorts compared to FMCC for each species.
One possible reason for this is because FMCC released smaller individuals than Harrison Lake. 
We would expect these smaller mussels to have a lower survival rate than older, larger mussels.
This effect was more pronounced for *A. varicosa*. 
While smaller sizes likely contributed to this, it is also possible that the small size and low recapture rate of the  FMCC release cohort of *A. varicosa* affected estimates of survival.
The model used for estimation did not allow detectability to vary between facilities.
However, of the 34 uniquely tagged mussels released by FMCC, we observed only 4 at least once over all sampling occasions, whereas 211 of the 245 Harrison Lake releases were observed.
It is more likely that the data for uniquely tagged, FMCC *A. varicosa* were too sparse for our selected model to estimate their detectability separately from Harrison Lake releases.
Given that the selected model assumes detectability is high and that survival estimates are linked to detectability, we expect that the true survival for FMCC *A. varicosa* is likely somewhat higher than estimated here. 
Essentially, if detectability is high and the data indicates very few mussels were observed, then survival must by necessity be low.

# Figures

![Apparent survival estimates from the selected POPAN model. Error bars indicate 95% CIs. Each row (i.e., species) represents results from a single analysis/model.](../../results/figures/07_reduced_model_results/Figure_1_survival.jpg){#fig-survival}

![Abundance estimates from the selected POPAN model, reported as a percentage of the initial release of uniquely tagged individuals. Error bars indicate 95% CIs. Each row (i.e., species) represents results from a single analysis/model.](../../results/figures/07_reduced_model_results/Figure_2_abundance_percent_release.jpg){#fig-abundance-percent}

![Abundance estimates of all individuals from the initial release (uniquely tagged, non-uniquely tagged, and untagged). Based on apparent survival estimates from the selected POPAN model and number of mussels in release cohorts. Each row (i.e., species) represents results from a single analysis/model.](../../results/figures/07_reduced_model_results/Figure_3_abundance_full_cohort.jpg){#fig-abundance-all}

# Tables

```{r}
#| label: tbl-abundance
#| tbl-cap: 'Abundance estimates from the selected POPAN model. Each row (i.e., species) represents results from a single analysis/model.'
#| ft.keepnext: false

make_abundance_table(tbl_abundance_summary)

```

\newpage
```{r}
#| label: tbl-abundance_all
#| tbl-cap: 'Abundance estimates of all individuals from the initial release (uniquely tagged, non-uniquely tagged, and untagged). Based on apparent survival estimates from the selected POPAN model and initial number of mussels released. Each row (i.e., species) represents results from a single analysis/model.'
#| ft.keepnext: false
make_abundance_table(tbl_abundance_all_summary)
```
